feedback.text: Parents should look at their children, instead of blaming private companies and other people for their mistakes.Think about when they give their phone, or their credit card data to their minor children and when their children purchase something, they blame the seller despite that didn't control what their kids were doing...In today's interconnected world, where technology and commerce are easily accessible, parents often face challenges in supervising their children's activities.A common scenario is when a child makes a purchase online or in an app using a parent's device or credit card information without explicit permission.When this happens, it's easy for parents to feel frustration and look for someone to blame, often pointing fingers at the companies that made the product or service available.However, a more productive and responsible approach is for parents to look inward and take responsibility for their own choices and actions.Parents are their children's first and most important teachers, mentors, and protectors.The responsibility of guiding children through the complexities of life, including financial decisions and digital etiquette, rests squarely on their shoulders.When parents provide their children with a smartphone or a credit card, they are granting them access to a world of potential pitfalls and opportunities.It is a parent's duty to establish clear boundaries, educate their children about responsible usage, and monitor their activities.Blaming a company for a purchase made with a shared device or card is akin to blaming a car manufacturer when a child gets into an accident because a parent didn't teach to that child that first of all a child is not supposed to drive and second, in the case of a teen or young adult it's their fault for not teaching how to drive. People, especially kids, doesn't randomly spawn on this world with all knowledge needed, an adult have to teach them.Companies design and market their products to a broad audience, and they cannot be expected to know or control who is using a parent's account.Parental supervision, not corporate oversight, is the key to preventing unwanted purchases and fostering responsible behavior.When parents shift the blame to external entities, they miss a valuable opportunity to teach their children about consequences, accountability, and digital literacy.By confronting the situation head-on, parents can help their children understand the value of money, the importance of asking for permission, and the potential negative outcomes of their actions.This process of teaching and learning is crucial for building a child's character and preparing them for adulthood. When children are allowed to face the consequences of their mistakes, they are more likely to learn from them and make better choices in the future.In contrast, when parents constantly intervene and deflect blame, they are inadvertently teaching their children that their actions have no real consequences and that others are always at fault. Taking responsibility for their children's mistakes is a powerful way for parents to model ethical behavior and strengthen the parent-child bond.Instead of blaming others, parents should focus on implementing practical solutions.This includes setting strong passwords, enabling parental controls, and having open and honest conversations with their children about technology and money.Ultimately, the burden of ensuring a child's well-being and responsible behavior falls on the parents. By accepting this responsibility, they empower themselves to be more effective guides and teachers, and they give their children the tools they need to navigate the world successfully.We shouldn't put excessive pressure or censorship on private companies just because bad parents allow their kids to avoid restriction, give them all their money, don't watch them and when they spend their money, or use their identity/account to play something, they blame someone else like an innocent seller...
